doc.content: BIS press release, May 13, 2025: 'Department of Commerce Announces Rescission of Biden-Era Artificial Intelligence Diffusion Rule.' The AI Diffusion Rule (interim final rule issued January 15, 2025, 90 FR 4544, Doc. 2025-00636; compliance requirements were to take effect May 15, 2025) is rescinded; Under Secretary Kessler instructed BIS enforcement officials not to enforce it. Reasons given (verbatim): the rule 'would have stifled American innovation and saddled companies with burdensome new regulatory requirements' and 'would have undermined U.S. diplomatic relations with dozens of countries by downgrading them to second-tier status.' Official quote (verbatim): 'The Trump Administration will pursue a bold, inclusive strategy to American AI technology with trusted foreign countries around the world, while keeping the technology out of the hands of our adversaries.' — Under Secretary Jeffery Kessler. Simultaneously, BIS announced three guidance actions strengthening AI-chip controls: (1) guidance alerting industry to risks of using PRC advanced computing ICs, 'including specific Huawei Ascend chips,' that were likely developed or produced in violation of U.S. export controls (General Prohibition 10 / GP10 guidance); (2) guidance warning about allowing U.S. AI chips to be used for training and inference of Chinese AI models (AI Model Training Policy Statement); (3) guidance to U.S. companies on protecting supply chains against diversion tactics (Diversion Guidance). BIS stated a replacement rule for the rescinded framework would be issued 'in the future.'

notes: BIS press release on rescission of the Biden-era AI Diffusion Rule (export-control policy, official post). A deep extraction captures three distinct export-control signals: the rescission of the tiered-country Diffusion Rule, the guidance against Huawei Ascend chips, and the policy statement on US-chip use for Chinese AI training. All are qualitative (observed, value=null) affecting export-control exposure; reward honest no-number extraction and correctly separating the three policy actions rather than collapsing them into one finding.
